title: Drawknife technique
subtitle: Using a drawknife at the shaving horse for green-wood shaping
excerpt: How to use a drawknife to rough-shape green-wood billets at the shaving horse. The drawknife removes stock faster than any hand knife and produces flat or convex surfaces in one long stroke. The primary shaping tool for stool legs, tool handles, and spoon blanks at scale.
sourceNotes: Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics vol. III for the drawknife method. Newey & Drage Practical Carpentry for shaving-horse setup and grip. The method is consistent with descriptions in the Museum of English Rural Life open-access archive on bodger and turner tools.

glossaryTerms.definition: A two-handled blade pulled toward the body along the workpiece; the green-woodwork rough-shaping tool.
A foot-pedalled bench that clamps a workpiece for two-handed drawknife and spokeshave work.
Freshly felled or recently riven wood, still high in moisture content. Carves easily under hand tools.
Split along the grain with a froe and maul rather than sawn.

content.text: The
drawknife
is a two-handled blade that is drawn toward you along the surface of the workpiece. At the
shaving horse
, where the foot pedal clamps the work securely, the drawknife can remove stock in long, continuous shavings faster than any other hand tool. It is the primary rough-shaping tool for
green-wood
projects: stool legs, mallet handles, spoon blanks, chair stiles, and drawknife-shaved shingles.
Before you start cutting.
Grip: four-finger grip on the work; thumb-push grip on the knife. Never make a cut whose blade-path can reach a body part.
Cut direction: push cuts (blade away from body) and thumb-pivot cuts are the default. Pull cuts only with the work braced against the chest and the blade-arc unable to reach anything vital.
Sharpening: a dull tool is more dangerous than a sharp one. Strop every fifteen minutes of carving, hone weekly, oilstone monthly.
First aid: keep a styptic pencil, gauze, and a clean dressing within reach. Apply firm pressure, elevate, and dress. A cut that shows fat or will not close with pressure goes to A&E, not a plaster.
Workspace: stable seat, good light, no children or pets within arm-and-blade reach.
When to use it
The drawknife is used for rough shaping of
riven

blanks before the knife or spokeshave takes over for finer work. It removes the outer sapwood from a freshly riven half-billet, squares up a rough section into a consistent taper, and flattens the back of a spoon blank. Do not use a drawknife on curved or interlocked grain: it will follow the grain rather than the intended line, and the cut will dive into the wood.
Setting up the shaving horse

The shaving horse is a foot-pedalled bench with a dumb-head clamp at the forward end. The work rests on the top of the bench; the dumb-head presses down onto it when the rider pushes the foot pedal forward. Sit with both feet on the pedal. The bench height should put the workpiece at about elbow height when you are sitting down.
The grip and the stroke

Hold the drawknife handles in both hands with a loose overhand grip, knuckles uppermost. The bevel of the blade should face down (bevel-down is the starting position for rough shaping; bevel-up gives a lighter, finer cut). The blade edge rests on the wood surface at the point where you want the cut to start.

Draw the knife toward you in a long smooth stroke, applying downward pressure at the start and easing it as the blade approaches your body. The depth of cut is controlled by the angle of the handles: raising the handles digs the edge in deeper; lowering them brings it shallower. For roughing cuts on green wood, a medium depth of 3 to 4 mm per stroke is usual.
Reading the grain

The drawknife follows the grain of the wood. If the grain dips away from you as you draw the blade, the cut runs cleanly. If the grain rises toward you, the blade dives below the surface and takes a deep, uncontrolled bite. Look at the grain lines on the side of the blank before each stroke. Work with the grain by moving your position or the workpiece if the grain direction goes against you.
What a correct cut looks like

A clean drawknife shaving is thin (under 1 mm), translucent, and lifts off in a long continuous strip. The surface left is smooth and slightly faceted. If the shaving is thick and the surface is rough and torn, the blade is going against the grain or the edge is dull. Strop and check the grain direction.
Common mistakes
Cut dives suddenly and takes too much wood: grain reversed mid-blank. Stop, reposition the blank or switch direction, and take a lighter cut to correct the step.
Work slipping in the shaving-horse clamp: foot pressure released mid-stroke. Keep the foot pedal fully depressed throughout the stroke. Release only to reposition the blank.
Edge skating off the surface rather than biting: drawknife dull or bevel angle wrong. Try bevel-up (handles tilted forward) for a lighter entry angle. Strop if skipping persists.
